nodes.label: MCP server (front desk: decides whether/what)
nodes.rationale: Owns protocol, tool schemas, validation/policy, warden invocation with an outer deadline, result shaping, audit logging, config. Never sets up namespaces, touches cgroups, or runs untrusted code in its own process.

nodes.label: Warden (isolation core: decides how)
nodes.rationale: 16-stage lifecycle: become root in a private world (userns), seal every dimension (namespaces, pivot_root, cgroups, seccomp), drop in, run the guest. Order matters: several operations are irreversible or must happen before a privilege boundary closes.

nodes.label: User-namespace root mapping (unshare CLONE_NEWUSER)
nodes.rationale: The key trick for unprivileged operation: the unprivileged real UID maps to UID 0 inside the namespace — full capabilities inside, zero outside; same mechanism as rootless Podman/bubblewrap. Footgun: write deny to setgroups before gid_map or the write fails EPERM.

nodes.label: Empty network namespace (no egress guarantee)
nodes.rationale: CLONE_NEWNET with no veth pair gives the guest only a down loopback interface. The no-network-egress guarantee is free — never bridge it to the host — and is stronger and simpler than firewall rules.

nodes.label: pivot_root filesystem jail
nodes.rationale: Guest sees only a read-only minimal rootfs, a size-capped tmpfs /tmp, and the single bind-mounted workspace. ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, .env are simply not present in the guest filesystem — exfiltration is structurally prevented, not policy-blocked.

nodes.label: cgroups v2 resource cage
nodes.rationale: memory.max OOMs the guest not the host; pids.max contains fork bombs; cpu.max quotas CPU. Just filesystem writes, no ctypes. Unprivileged control needs v2 delegation — degrade gracefully and report it in isolation, don't hard-fail.

nodes.label: seccomp-bpf syscall allowlist
nodes.rationale: Defense-in-depth on top of namespaces + cgroups, kept optional/degradable via pyseccomp — never hand-assemble BPF. Ordering: no_new_privs before seccomp; seccomp last, right before execve, so setup code isn't caught by the filter.

nodes.label: Two nested timeouts (inner authoritative, outer backstop)
nodes.rationale: Inner timeout: the warden kills the guest (authoritative). Outer: asyncio.wait_for protects the server from a wedged warden. Wall-clock enforcement is separate from CPU limits because cpu caps alone don't catch sleep(99999).

nodes.label: Tool schemas as UX for the model
nodes.rationale: The user of the schema is an LLM reading description fields. Descriptions are prompts; enums/bounds make invalid states unrepresentable; failures must be legible content ('Blocked: ...') so the model reads them and adapts.

nodes.label: Honest scope: kernel-sharing isolation, not a VM boundary
nodes.rationale: Isolation is comparable in mechanism to rootless containers but shares the host kernel, so a kernel privilege-escalation exploit could escape. Goal: make common, realistic agent risks structurally impossible; stating limits honestly is a trust-builder. Never claim 'unescapable'.
